differential.evidence_basis: Sudden bilateral intense itch with watery discharge after outdoor allergen exposure is the classic ocular-allergy pattern; itch is the dominant symptom.
Sudden periorbital/facial swelling mandates an active airway/systemic screen before benign closure; must be excluded, not assumed.
Periorbital swelling raises orbital cellulitis; excluded by bilateral symmetry, absence of pain/fever, and full painless eye movements.
A painful red eye with vision loss/photophobia would suggest these; screened out here by absence of pain, photophobia and visual change.
Also causes bilateral watery red eyes, but itch-predominant explosive onset with rhinitis favours allergy over a gritty viral course.
Strictly clear watery discharge with no purulence or lash matting argues strongly against bacterial infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI should hold allergic conjunctivitis as leading WHILE actively screening the important-not-to-miss mimics (anaphylaxis, orbital cellulitis, sight-threatening red-eye) rather than closing prematurely on either the benign or the dramatic extreme.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral itch-predominant watery presentation with concurrent rhinitis, no airway involvement, no pain/photophobia/vision change, no fever — the allergic pattern is confirmed clinically.
No lip/tongue/throat swelling, no dyspnoea, no widespread urticaria, no faintness — no systemic or airway involvement.
Bilateral and symmetric, painless, afebrile, with full painless eye movements — inconsistent with orbital cellulitis (typically unilateral, painful, febrile).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the targeted red-flag screen is negative, the correct move is to de-escalate confidently to the benign diagnosis and NOT to keep the patient at a high tier out of anchoring on the initial dramatic swelling.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed benign IgE-mediated ocular allergy with concurrent allergic rhinitis; self-limiting and telehealth/pharmacist-manageable with a robust safety-net for the excluded mimics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is a low-acuity allergic diagnosis. The test is correct recognition + low tier + safety-netting; both over-triage (ED/000) and dismissal without safety-net are failures.

red_flags.description: Airway / systemic allergic involvement — lip, tongue or throat swelling, difficulty breathing, widespread hives, or faintness (anaphylaxis/angioedema)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Given sudden facial/periorbital swelling, anaphylaxis is the one time-critical mimic; if present it is an immediate life threat requiring adrenaline and 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed evolving anaphylaxis can progress to airway occlusion and shock within minutes.

red_flags.description: Unilateral painful proptotic eye with fever or pain on eye movement (orbital cellulitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orbital cellulitis is a sight- and life-threatening emergency; bilateral painless symmetry with no fever makes it very unlikely here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated orbital cellulitis can cause vision loss, cavernous sinus thrombosis and intracranial spread.

red_flags.description: Vision loss, severe deep/aching ocular pain, photophobia or haloes (keratitis, uveitis, acute angle-closure glaucoma)
red_flags.clinical_significance: These features flag a sight-threatening red eye needing same-day ophthalmology; their absence supports a benign surface allergy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed treatment of acute glaucoma or keratitis risks permanent vision loss.

red_flags.description: Thick yellow/green purulent discharge with lash matting (bacterial/gonococcal keratoconjunctivitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Purulence would shift toward bacterial infection needing antibiotics and, if hyperacute, urgent ophthalmology.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed gonococcal keratoconjunctivitis can perforate the cornea; ordinary bacterial infection is milder but needs different treatment.

pathophysiology_summary: Re-exposure to an aeroallergen (pollen while outdoors) cross-links IgE on conjunctival and nasal mast cells, triggering type I hypersensitivity degranulation. Released histamine and other mediators produce the cardinal itch, conjunctival vasodilation (hyperaemia), and increased vascular permeability causing conjunctival oedema (chemosis) and eyelid/periorbital swelling, with reflex watery (serous) discharge. A tarsal papillary reaction reflects local immune inflammation. The same mediator release in the nasal mucosa drives concurrent sneezing and clear rhinorrhoea (allergic rhinoconjunctivitis, the 'united airway'). The process is self-limiting once the allergen is removed and is reversed pharmacologically by topical antihistamine/mast-cell stabilisers; it does NOT involve airway, cornea or orbit, which is what separates it from the dangerous mimics.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being driven by the dramatic 'eyes swollen shut' picture into treating a benign allergic eye as an emergency and escalating to ED/000 when the airway and vision are intact.
Jumping to the benign allergic diagnosis without explicitly excluding lip/tongue/throat swelling and breathing difficulty — the airway screen is mandatory whenever facial swelling is sudden.
Labelling a clear-watery, itch-predominant bilateral eye as bacterial conjunctivitis and prescribing antibiotic drops.
Failing to check laterality, eye-movement pain, proptosis and fever, and thereby missing a unilateral orbital cellulitis in a different patient with similar-sounding swelling.
Initiating topical corticosteroid or vasoconstrictor drops remotely without slit-lamp exclusion of herpetic keratitis or IOP effects — outside safe telehealth scope.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Slit-lamp examination of the cornea and anterior chamber
Fluorescein staining to exclude corneal ulcer/abrasion or herpetic dendrite
Eyelid eversion to grade the tarsal papillary reaction
Objective grading of chemosis and periorbital oedema
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: For THIS case — airway clear, no pain/photophobia/vision change, bilateral, afebrile — telehealth self-care and pharmacological advice (topical antihistamine/mast-cell stabiliser + cold compresses + allergen avoidance) with explicit safety-netting; escalate to 000 or ED only if a red flag (RF-001 to RF-004) appears.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign diagnosis (the constellation is classic). The real risks are (a) UNDER-screening — assuming benign allergy without excluding anaphylaxis given the sudden swelling — and (b) OVER-triage — sending a benign allergic eye to ED/000 on the strength of the dramatic presentation.
